welcome.title: Welcome to Wrench Board.
welcome.body: I'm your bench assistant. Tell me a device and a symptom, and I'll build the knowledge sheet, then walk the diagnostic with you: board view, schematic, causal reasoning, the lot.
welcome.skip: Skip, I'll explore on my own

profile.bubble: First, let's set you up. A couple of quick steps so I can match my help to you and your bench.
profile.name_required: Your name is required to continue.

wizard.identity_intro: So I can match my tone to your level.
wizard.workshop_intro: Tick what you have. I'll only suggest moves your bench can do.
wizard.posture_intro: Set how much I explain. You can change this anytime.

level.desc_auto: I'll infer your level from the skills you log over time.

posture.desc_auto: I adapt the depth to your level.
posture.desc_concise: Short answers, just the next move.
posture.desc_teaching: I explain the why behind every step, like a mentor.

concept.bubble: Here's the idea: each repair is a persistent session, like a ticket you can leave and come back to. When you name a device, I research it, build its knowledge sheet, then open the diagnostic right here.

example.bubble: Want to see it on a real board first? I've got a full example on hand: the MNT Reform motherboard. Let me give you a quick tour, then you'll run your own.

firstdiag.bubble_device: Let's try one. Drop a device here. A model name is enough, I'll find the rest.
firstdiag.bubble_symptom: Now the symptom: what's the board doing wrong?
firstdiag.bubble_launch: That's all I need. Hit start and watch me learn the device, phase by phase. Or tap a suggestion below.

finish.bubble: You're set. I'll be right here whenever you open a repair. Good luck on the bench.

modal.title: Your profile
modal.subtitle: Tune how I work with you.

knowledge.title: Add knowledge
knowledge.intro: Feed the device its own sources so the agent can reason about the real board, not just generic knowledge.
knowledge.p1: Schematic PDF: parsed page by page into an electrical graph. Unlocks the simulator, mb_hypothesize and rail-level causal reasoning.
knowledge.p2: Boardview (.brd / .kicad_pcb / .fz): unlocks pad-level highlighting, net tracing and component focus on the 3D board.
knowledge.p3: Without them the agent still helps, but it can't reason about this board's causality. Add what you have, even just one.

stock.title: Donor stock
stock.intro: Search replacement parts across your donor boards instead of ordering, with the agent picking only safe equivalents.
stock.p1: Works on devices with an analyzed pack (an electrical graph): parts are typed, valued and safety-classified into a searchable index.
stock.p2: Mark dead boards as donors. Their parts become searchable; consuming a part logs it so your inventory stays accurate.
stock.p3: Potential gain: reuse a part you already own in seconds, find equivalents you didn't think of, cut order delays.
stock.p4: With the agent: once a root cause needs a replacement, it calls stock_search under a hard safety filter (refuses tuned / feedback / RF / SMPS / IC roles) and proposes donor matches.

repair.title: How a repair works
repair.intro: A repair is a persistent session: I learn your device, then walk the diagnostic with you.
repair.p1: Knowledge sheet: when you name a device I research it and build its electrical knowledge base (the timeline you saw on launch).
repair.p2: Views: the rail on the left switches between the diagnostic dashboard, the PCB boardview, the schematic and the knowledge graph.
repair.p3: Capabilities grow with data: load a schematic or boardview and I unlock the simulator, hypotheses and pad-level reasoning.
repair.p4: Diagnose by talking: describe the symptom and your measurements in the chat. I propose protocols, suspects and the next step, logging findings as we go.

tour.knowledge: See this? Add a schematic or board file here to unlock the simulator and pad-level reasoning. Tap the ? anytime.
tour.stock: And up here: your donor stock. Reuse parts from dead boards instead of ordering. Tap the ? to see how.

coach.session: You're in a repair now: a persistent session you can leave and come back to anytime. I've just analyzed this device: schematic, components, power rails, all in memory.
coach.cap: Here's what I can do on this board right now. The more you give me (a schematic, a boardview), the higher the score climbs and the more tools unlock.
coach.cards: The device data I've gathered. You can add to it (a schematic or a boardview) to go deeper.
coach.pcb: PCB view: your actual board. Highlight a part and I isolate its pads, its net and what sits around it.
coach.demo_pcb: PCB view: the real MNT Reform. I've lit up U14, the regulator that puts out the standby 3V3 rail, typically the first part you probe when a board won't power on.
coach.schematic: Schematic view: the navigable electrical schematic, the base for the simulator and fault hypotheses.
coach.demo_schematic: Schematic view: I click the LPC_VCC rail (the standby supply). The schematic isolates the rail and its whole cascade. That's how I trace a fault back to its cause.
coach.graph: Memory view: the whole board as a graph (components, links and the rules I've inferred).
coach.demo_graph: Memory view: everything I know about this board, as a graph. I click a node: the right-hand panel unfolds the detail (what it is, the confidence, and how it links to the rest).
coach.demo_graph_raw: And the Raw tab: the same memory as text (rules, facts, observations). That's my raw material, this is what I reason over to diagnose.
coach.chat: To diagnose, just talk to me: describe the symptom and your measurements, and I'll guide you step by step.
coach.demo_chat: The heart of it: you describe your fault, the agent diagnoses. Watch a real case, step by step.
coach.demo_q1: Walk me through this board's power-up sequence: which rails come up, in what order. Show it on the board.
coach.demo_c1_explore: You ask your question. The agent reads what it already knows about this board: schematic, components, power rails.
coach.demo_c1_board: It draws the sequence right on the board: the power cascade, from the input connector all the way to the CPU module, with arrows and labels.
coach.demo_c1_done: And it explains it phase by phase: which regulator wakes which rail, in what order. That's what knowing a board looks like.
coach.demo_switch: Now watch: I open the conversation switcher and create a new one. The previous thread is set aside and the board clears, but the agent keeps this device in memory.
coach.demo_q2: The board is dead: no rail, no LED. Diagnose it and give me a measurement protocol.
coach.demo_c2_diag: New conversation, new fault: "dead board". The agent explores the memory, validates the components, and lays out its reasoning: the input chain J1 → F1 → VIN → U14.
coach.demo_c2_protocol: It draws the chain on the board and proposes a 6-step measurement protocol. Safety first: we start cold, nothing plugged in.
coach.demo_c2_cold: Cold and risk-free: fuse F1 is good, and there's no short on VIN. Two leads ruled out.
coach.demo_c2_power: Now powered: the 24 V from the adapter does reach the board.
coach.demo_c2_u14: VIN is healthy all the way to the system bus → the agent pivots its suspect to U14, the 3.3 V standby regulator, and shows you where to probe.
coach.demo_c2_verdict: The twist: U14 does output 3.3 V. Every supply rail is healthy! It's not a dead board, it's a sequencing issue. Measure, then revise the hypothesis: that's a good diagnostic.
coach.demo_after: That's a full diagnostic: reasoning, board annotations, a measurement protocol, a verdict. Your turn: describe your fault and I'll do the same on your board.
